name: Kashi Vishwanath Temple
description: One of the most sacred Shiva temples and a Jyotirlinga. Rebuilt by Queen Ahilyabai Holkar in 1780. Close to the Ganges, Kashi's spiritual center.
history: The Kashi Vishwanath Temple has a long and turbulent history, reflecting the resilience of Indian spiritual heritage. While the original temple's date of construction is lost in antiquity, historical records show it was a significant site as far back as the 11th century. Over the following centuries, the temple was destroyed multiple times during various invasions, most notably by the Delhi Sultanate and later by the Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb, who built the Gyanvapi Mosque on its original site. The current structure was finally built in 1780 by Maharani Ahilyabai Holkar of Indore, a great devotee of Lord Shiva. Later, in 1835, Maharaja Ranjit Singh of Punjab donated 1,000 kg of pure gold to plate the temple's domes, earning it the famous name "The Golden Temple."
mythology: According to sacred Hindu scriptures, Varanasi (Kashi) is the oldest living city in the world and was founded by Lord Shiva himself. Mythology states that the Kashi Vishwanath Jyotirlinga is the first and most powerful of the twelve Jyotirlingas. It is said that when a fiery pillar of light (the Jyotirlinga) pierced through the earth to settle a dispute between Lord Brahma and Lord Vishnu regarding their supremacy, it emerged at this very spot. Devotees believe that Kashi stands on the tip of Shiva's Trishul (trident) and is not part of the earthly realm. It is widely held that anyone who breathes their last here attains 'Moksha' (liberation from the cycle of birth and death), as Lord Shiva himself whispers the 'Taraka Mantra' into the ears of the dying,

name: Kedarnath Temple
description: Located high in the Garhwal Himalayas, Kedarnath is part of Char Dham. Revived by Adi Shankaracharya. Survived 2013 floods.
history: The Kedarnath temple is an architectural marvel built of extremely large, heavy, and evenly cut grey stone slabs. While the origin of the temple is ancient, the current structure is widely credited to the great philosopher Adi Shankaracharya in the 8th century, who revived the site. The temple has stood the test of time and extreme weather for over a thousand years, most notably surviving the catastrophic 2013 North India floods. While the entire town around the temple was washed away, the temple structure remained largely intact, an event many devotees consider a modern-day miracle.
mythology: The mythology of Kedarnath is deeply tied to the Mahabharata. It is said that after the Kurukshetra war, the Pandavas sought the blessings of Lord Shiva to atone for the sins of fratricide. Shiva, wishing to avoid them, took the form of a bull and hid in the ground at Kedarnath. When Bhima tried to hold him, the bull dived into the earth, leaving only its hump on the surface. This hump is what is worshipped today as the deity in the temple. It is one of the twelve Jyotirlingas and the highest among them, representing Shiva as the 'Lord of the Field'.

name: Badrinath Temple
description: One of the Char Dham temples dedicated to Vishnu. Closes during winter due to heavy snow.
history: The Badrinath temple is located along the banks of the Alaknanda River in the hill town of Badrinath. While mentions of the site exist in ancient Vedic texts, the current temple is said to have been established as a major pilgrimage site by Adi Shankaracharya in the 8th century. Historical records indicate that Shankaracharya discovered the idol of Lord Badri in the Alaknanda River and enshrined it in a cave near the Tapt Kund hot springs. Over the centuries, the temple has undergone several major renovations by the Kings of Garhwal and the Scindias of Gwalior. The architecture is unique, featuring a brightly painted facade that resembles a Buddhist monastery, a nod to the region's diverse cultural history.
mythology: According to Hindu mythology, Lord Vishnu sat in deep meditation at this spot. His consort, Goddess Lakshmi, took the form of a 'Badri' (Jujube) tree to provide him shelter from the harsh Himalayan weather, which is why the deity is known as Badrinath (Lord of the Badri). Another popular legend states that the Pandavas passed through Badrinath on their final journey to heaven (Swargarohini). It is also believed that the sage Vyasa wrote the Mahabharata in a cave nearby with the help of Lord Ganesha. The temple is considered one of the most sacred 'Divya Desams' and is the only shrine that is part of both the 'Chota' Char Dham and the 'Bada' Char Dham circuits.

name: Gangotri Temple
description: Marks the origin of the sacred Ganges River. Part of Char Dham.
history: The Gangotri Temple has a rich history, with the current structure being built in the 18th century. It has been destroyed and rebuilt multiple times throughout history, particularly during the medieval period when it faced attacks from various invaders. The temple was later restored by local rulers including the Paramara King Bhoj and the Solanki King Kumarpal. The present structure was built in the Chalukya style of architecture and completed in 1951 under the leadership of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, symbolizing the spirit of a new India.
mythology: According to Hindu mythology, the Gangotri Temple is dedicated to Goddess Ganga, who descended from heaven to earth. It is believed that the river Ganges was born here, making it one of the most sacred sites in Hinduism.

name: Yamunotri Temple
description: Dedicated to the Yamuna River goddess. Part of Char Dham pilgrimage.
history: The Yamunotri temple, located in the Uttarkashi district of Uttarakhand, is the first stop in the sacred Char Dham pilgrimage. The temple was originally constructed in the 19th century by Maharani Gularia of Jaipur and later rebuilt by Maharaja Pratap Shah of Tehri Garhwal. Due to its high altitude and proximity to glaciers, the temple has been destroyed several times by snow and floods, leading to multiple renovations. Nearby, the 'Tapt Kund' hot springs serve as a place where pilgrims cook rice and potatoes as 'prasad' in the boiling water before offering it at the shrine.
mythology: According to Hindu mythology, the Yamunotri temple is dedicated to the goddess Yamuna, who is believed to have descended from heaven to earth. It is said that the river Yamuna was born here, making it one of the most sacred sites in Hinduism.

name: Chitrakoot Temples
description: Sacred sites for Lord Ram and Sita, with multiple temples along the banks of Mandakini River.
history: Chitrakoot, spread across the borders of Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh, is one of India's most ancient spiritual centers. It has been a retreat for sages and seers since the Vedic period. Historically, the various ghats and temples were built and patronized by the Bundela kings and later Maratha rulers. The region is dotted with ancient caves and ashrams that have preserved the spiritual atmosphere for millennia. The 'Ram Bharat Milap' temple specifically commemorates the emotional reunion of the brothers during Lord Rama's exile, standing as a monument to familial love and duty.
mythology: In the Ramayana, Chitrakoot is the place where Lord Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana spent 11 years of their 14-year exile. Mythology states that the mountain itself is sacred and that all the gods and goddesses came to Chitrakoot when Rama was staying here. The 'Bharat Milap' spot is where Bharat came to plead with Rama to return to Ayodhya. It is said that the meeting was so emotional that even the rocks and mountains of Chitrakoot melted in grief, leaving behind the footprints of the two brothers which are still worshipped by devotees today.

name: Somnath Temple
description: Believed to be the first Jyotirlinga of Shiva. Rebuilt multiple times throughout history.
history: The Somnath Temple is known as the 'Shrine Eternal' because it has been destroyed and rebuilt at least six times throughout history. Archaeological findings suggest that a temple existed here as early as the first millennium. The temple faced repeated attacks from invaders, most famously by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1024 CE, and later by the Delhi Sultanate and the Mughals. Each time it was razed, it was reconstructed by local kings including the Paramara King Bhoj and the Solanki King Kumarpal. The present structure was built in the Chalukya style of architecture and completed in 1951 under the leadership of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, symbolizing the spirit of a new India.
mythology: According to the Puranas, the temple was originally built by Chandra (the Moon God) himself. Legend says that Chandra was married to the 27 daughters of Daksha Prajapati, but he only loved Rohini. In anger, Daksha cursed Chandra to lose his luster and fade away. To regain his light, Chandra bathed in the Saraswati River at Prabhas Patan and prayed to Lord Shiva. Shiva, pleased by his penance, partially cured the curse, resulting in the waxing and waning of the moon. In gratitude, Chandra built a golden temple for Shiva. It is said that the temple was later rebuilt in silver by Ravana, in wood by Krishna, and in stone by King Bhimdev.

name: Mahakaleshwar Temple
description: A Jyotirlinga temple famous for the Bhasma Aarti ritual performed with sacred ash.
history: The Mahakaleshwar temple is one of the most ancient and revered Jyotirlingas in India. Historically, Ujjain was a major political and spiritual hub. The temple was destroyed by Sultan Iltutmish during his raid of Ujjain in the 13th century. For nearly five hundred years, the site remained in a state of ruin until it was reconstructed in the 18th century by the Scindias, the Maratha rulers of Gwalior. The temple is famous for the 'Bhasma Aarti,' where the deity is worshipped with sacred ash, a ritual that has continued for centuries through various political eras, symbolizing the eternal nature of life and death.
mythology: Mythology states that Lord Shiva appeared in Ujjain to protect his devotees from a demon named Dushana who was terrorizing the residents. After slaying the demon, Shiva took the form of Mahakala (the Lord of Time and Death) and chose to reside here eternally. Unlike all other Jyotirlingas, the idol here is 'Dakshinamurti,' meaning it faces south—the direction associated with death. This signifies that Shiva is the supreme master of time and the only power that can conquer death. The city of Ujjain is also one of the four places where drops of nectar fell during the Samudra Manthan.

name: Omkareshwar Temple
description: Situated on an island in the Narmada River shaped like the sacred Om symbol.
history: The Omkareshwar temple is situated on Mandhata island in the Narmada River, Madhya Pradesh. The island's geography is naturally shaped like the sacred symbol 'Om.' While the core of the temple is ancient, much of its grand structure was renovated and expanded during the Maratha period. The architecture features massive carved pillars made of soft stone, displaying intricate geometric and floral patterns. Historically, it has been a major center for Vedic learning and meditation, surviving through various dynastic shifts in Central India due to its unique island location.
mythology: According to the Puranas, the Vindhya Mountain performed severe penance to please Lord Shiva to wash away its sins. Shiva was pleased and appeared in two forms: Omkareshwar (the Lord of the Om sound) and Mamleshwar. Another legend says that King Mandhata performed great sacrifices here, leading the island to be named after him. It is believed that Lord Shiva comes to this island every night to rest, which is why a special 'Shayan Aarti' (night ritual) is performed with a board game (Chaupar) placed for the Lord to play.

name: Trimbakeshwar Temple
description: One of the sacred Jyotirlingas, known for the origin of the Godavari River.
history: The Trimbakeshwar temple, located in the Nashik district of Maharashtra, was built in its current form by Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao in the mid-18th century. The temple is constructed entirely of black stone and is famous for its stunning Nagara-style architecture. It is situated at the foot of the Brahmagiri hills, which is the source of the Godavari River. The temple features a unique reservoir called 'Kushavarta,' where the river takes its first dip on earth. Historically, it has been a site for special Vedic rituals like Narayan Nagbali, attracting people from all over India for ancestral rites.
mythology: According to the Puranas, the Trimbakeshwar temple is associated with the origin of the Godavari River. It is believed that Lord Shiva appeared here to bless the region with this sacred river. The temple is also linked to the legend of Sage Agastya, who is said to have performed penance here to please Lord Shiva.

name: Bhimashankar Temple
description: Located in the Sahyadri hills, surrounded by dense forests and part of a wildlife sanctuary.
history: The Bhimashankar temple is a beautiful mix of old and new Nagara-style architecture. While the original temple is centuries old, the current structure and its grand mandapa were expanded by Nana Phadnavis, an influential Maratha minister, in the 18th century. The temple is built from dark black stone and features intricate carvings of human figures and scenes from the Puranas. It is also the source of the Bhima River, and the surrounding dense forests have been declared a wildlife sanctuary, preserving the natural beauty of this sacred site.
mythology: According to the Shiva Purana, Lord Shiva took the form of Bhimashankar to destroy a demon named Tripurasura. In another legend, it is said that a demon named Bhima (son of Kumbhakarna) terrorized the region. Upon the request of the gods, Shiva appeared and reduced the demon to ashes. The sweat from Shiva's body after the battle is said to have formed the Bhima River. This Jyotirlinga is considered one of the most powerful symbols of Shiva's protective energy against evil forces.

name: Grishneshwar Temple
description: Close to the famous Ellora Caves, it is considered the last Jyotirlinga.
history: Grishneshwar is the 12th and final Jyotirlinga of Lord Shiva. The temple is located near the famous Ellora Caves. Like many Indian temples, it suffered destruction during the Delhi Sultanate's rule. The temple we see today was rebuilt in its current red-volcanic stone form by the great Maratha queen, Maharani Ahilyabai Holkar of Indore, in the 18th century. It is a stunning example of Maratha-style architecture, featuring a five-tier shikhara and beautiful carvings that reflect the spiritual resilience of the region.
mythology: The mythology centers around a pious woman named Kusuma, a great devotee of Shiva. Her son was killed by her jealous sister-in-law and thrown into a lake. Even after hearing the news, Kusuma continued her daily ritual of making and worshipping clay Lingas. Impressed by her unwavering faith, Lord Shiva restored her son's life and appeared before her. Kusuma requested Shiva to stay there eternally for the benefit of all devotees, and thus He manifested as the Grishneshwar (Lord of Compassion) Jyotirlinga.

name: Tungnath Temple
description: Believed to be the highest Shiva temple in the world.
history: Tungnath is the highest Shiva temple in the world, situated at an altitude of 12,073 feet. It is part of the 'Panch Kedar' circuit. The temple is built in the classic North Indian style with stone slabs and a small wooden porch. Historically, the temple is believed to be over 1,000 years old. Due to its extreme altitude and heavy snowfall, the temple is only accessible during the summer months. It remains a marvel of ancient engineering, standing firm against the harsh Himalayan climate on a steep mountain ridge.
mythology: Legend has it that the Pandavas built this temple to appease Lord Shiva. After the war of Mahabharata, the Pandavas were looking for Shiva to forgive their sins. Shiva, wanting to avoid them, took the form of a bull and went underground. Parts of his body reappeared at five different places (Panch Kedar). Tungnath is where his hands (Bahu) are said to have emerged. The name 'Tungnath' literally translates to 'Lord of the Peaks,' representing the divine presence at the highest point of the earth.

name: Vaidyanath / Parli Vaijnath Temple
description: Historic Jyotirlinga temple known for its architectural beauty and spiritual importance.
history: Vaidyanath is one of the twelve Jyotirlingas of Lord Shiva. The temple is believed to be over 1,000 years old and is known for its unique architecture and spiritual significance. It is said that the temple was originally built by the Pandavas and later renovated by various dynasties.
mythology: According to legend, the temple was built by the Pandavas to appease Lord Shiva. It is believed that the deity appeared in the form of a lingam here, and the temple has been a place of pilgrimage for centuries.

name: Vaishno Devi Temple
description: One of the most famous Shakti Peethas, attracting millions of pilgrims every year.
history: The holy cave of Vaishno Devi is located at an altitude of 5,200 feet on the Trikuta Mountains. Unlike a traditional temple with idols, the Goddess is worshipped here in the form of three natural rock formations called 'Pindis.' The cave has been a center of pilgrimage for thousands of years, with mentions in the Mahabharata. In modern times, the Shri Mata Vaishno Devi Shrine Board has developed the route, making it one of the most organized and visited religious sites in India, attracting millions of pilgrims every year.
mythology: The Goddess Vaishno Devi was born as a human named Vaishnavi to destroy evil. She was pursued by a tantric named Bhairon Nath, who wanted to test her divine powers. To escape him, she moved through the mountains and meditated in a cave called Ardhkuwari for nine months. Finally, at the main cave, she took her divine form and beheaded Bhairon Nath. As he died, he realized her divinity and asked for forgiveness. She granted him a boon that no pilgrimage would be complete without visiting his temple, located slightly higher than her cave.

name: Chintpurni Temple
description: A famous Shakti Peetha where devotees come to offer prayers to the goddess for peace of mind.
history: Chintpurni is a major Shakti Peetha located in the Una district. The temple is dedicated to Mata Chhinnamasta (the headless Goddess). Historically, the temple was established by a Brahmin named Mai Das. The temple structure is relatively small but highly sacred, featuring an ancient banyan tree in the courtyard where devotees tie threads to make wishes. The location provides a serene view of the Shivalik ranges and has been a site of intense spiritual practice for centuries.
mythology: According to mythology, when Lord Shiva carried the body of Sati, her feet fell at this location. The deity is worshipped as Chhinnamasta, who cut off her own head to satisfy the bloodthirst of her attendants, Jaya and Vijaya, symbolizing the ultimate sacrifice and the cutting away of the ego. The name 'Chintpurni' means 'the one who fulfills all worries' or 'remover of anxieties.' It is believed that anyone who prays here with a pure heart is relieved of all their worldly stresses and troubles.

name: Mahalakshmi Temple
description: A popular Shakti temple, dedicated to the goddess of wealth and prosperity.
history: The Mahalakshmi Temple of Kolhapur is one of the six Shakti Peethas mentioned in the Puranas. The temple was built in the 7th century by the Chalukya dynasty and was later expanded by the Silahara and Yadava kings. The structure is built of stone and is famous for its unique 'Kiranotsav' (Festival of Sunrays), where once a year, the sun's rays fall directly on the feet, waist, and face of the deity through a small window. It has survived several invasions and stands as a major spiritual center in Maharashtra.
mythology: Legend says that Goddess Mahalakshmi chose to reside in Kolhapur after a dispute with Lord Vishnu. It is believed that Kolhapur is one of the places where a part of Sati's body fell. According to local lore, the Goddess defeated a demon named Kolhasura here, who, before dying, requested that the city be named after him. The deity is carved in black stone and is unique because it faces west, unlike most Hindu idols that face east or north.

name: Vindhyavasini Temple
description: A Shakti Peetha temple revered by devotees, located on the Vindhya hills.
history: Located on the banks of the Ganges in Uttar Pradesh, this temple is a significant Shakti Peetha. The temple is built on a small hill in the Vindhya range. Historically, it has been a center for Tantric worship and has been mentioned in ancient texts like the Markandeya Purana. The temple has seen various renovations by local kings over centuries and remains a massive pilgrimage hub during Navratri, attracting millions of devotees who perform the 'Vindhyachal Parikrama'.
mythology: According to the Durga Saptashati, the Goddess Vindhyavasini is the sister of Lord Krishna, who was swapped with him at birth to protect him from Kansa. When Kansa tried to kill the infant, she transformed into her divine form, warned him of his death, and settled in the Vindhya mountains. She is believed to be the 'Kajala' form of Shakti, providing protection and fulfilling the desires of all who seek her refuge in these sacred hills.

name: Jagannath Temple
description: Built by King Anantavarman Chodaganga Deva. Famous for Rath Yatra festival.
history: The Jagannath Temple is a masterpiece of Kalinga architecture, constructed in the 12th century by King Anantavarman Chodaganga Deva. The temple is famous for its massive outer walls and the world's largest kitchen, where 'Mahaprasad' is cooked using traditional methods. Historically, the temple has stood as a symbol of resistance, surviving 18 invasions over centuries. The architecture is scientifically advanced, with the temple shadow never falling on the ground at any time of the day.
mythology: The mythology of Jagannath is unique as the idols are made of wood. Legend says that the Lord appeared in a dream to King Indradyumna, asking him to carve the idols from a divine log found in the sea. The celestial architect Vishwakarma took the form of an old carpenter to carve them. The temple is most famous for the Ratha Yatra, where Lord Jagannath, Balabhadra, and Subhadra visit their birthplace at the Gundicha Temple in massive chariots.

name: Dwarkadhish Temple
description: Major Krishna pilgrimage site marking ancient kingdom of Krishna.
history: The Dwarkadhish Temple, also known as Jagat Mandir, is a 2,500-year-old site, though the current 5-story limestone structure dates back to the 16th century. It is built in the Solanki style and is part of the Char Dham pilgrimage. Archaeological excavations near the coast of Dwarka have found remnants of a submerged city, lending historical weight to the belief that this was once the capital of Krishna's kingdom before it was swallowed by the sea after his departure.
mythology: Mythology states that Lord Krishna established Dwarka as his capital after leaving Mathura. The city was built by the divine architect Vishwakarma on land reclaimed from the sea. It is said that when Krishna left his mortal body at the end of Dwapara Yuga, the entire city of gold sank into the Arabian Sea. The temple is believed to have been built by Krishna’s grandson, Vajranabha, over the site of the Lord's private palace, the 'Hari Griha'.

name: Krishna Janmabhoomi Temple
description: Birthplace of Lord Krishna in Mathura. Multiple structures over centuries.
history: The Krishna Janmabhoomi temple complex marks the exact birthplace of Lord Krishna. Over the centuries, several temples were built on this site, starting from the one built by King Vajranabha. The temple was destroyed by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1017 and later by Aurangzeb in 1670. The modern temple complex was rebuilt in the 20th century under the guidance of industrialist families and spiritual leaders, restoring the grandeur of this ancient pilgrimage site.
mythology: Mythology identifies this spot as the 'Katra Keshavdev,' the prison cell where King Kansa had imprisoned his sister Devaki and brother-in-law Vasudeva. Krishna was born here at midnight on Ashtami to destroy the tyrant Kansa. The divine infant was miraculously transported across the Yamuna to Gokul to keep him safe. Today, the small prison cell area is preserved within the temple, serving as the most sacred spot for Krishna devotees worldwide.

name: Banke Bihari Temple
description: Famous Krishna temple in Vrindavan. Idol in playful tribhanga posture.
history: The Banke Bihari Temple was established by the great saint and musician Swami Haridas in the 1860s. Swami Haridas was the guru of Tansen. Initially, the deity was worshipped in Nidhivan, but later the current temple was constructed to house the idol. The temple follows a unique tradition where there are no bells or loud instruments, and the curtain of the sanctum is pulled and opened every few minutes to ensure the Lord doesn't get tired from the continuous gaze of his devotees.
mythology: Legend says that Swami Haridas’s devotion was so intense that Lord Krishna and Radha appeared before him in a combined form of immense beauty. Fearing that common people would be overwhelmed by the divine light, Haridas requested them to take a single form, which became the idol of Banke Bihari. The name 'Banke' means 'bent in three places' (the flute-playing posture), and 'Bihari' means 'the supreme enjoyer'.

name: Prem Mandir
description: Modern temple entirely of white marble. Famous for carvings depicting Krishna's pastimes.
history: Prem Mandir is a modern architectural marvel, established by Jagadguru Shri Kripalu Ji Maharaj in 2001 and opened to the public in 2012. The temple is built entirely of Italian white Carrara marble. It features intricate hand-carved panels depicting the pastimes of Radha-Krishna and Ram-Sita. The temple complex is famous for its musical fountain and the lighting system that changes colors every few seconds, making it one of the most visited sites in modern Vrindavan.
mythology: The temple is dedicated to 'Divine Love' (Prem). While it is a modern structure, it is built on the philosophy of the eternal love between the soul and God. The statues and dioramas inside the temple grounds depict the 'Ras Leela' and various other events from the Bhagavata Purana, aiming to educate visitors about the spiritual stages of devotion through the medium of art and beauty.

name: Radha Raman Temple
description: Established by Gopal Bhatta Goswami, featuring self-manifested Shaligram stone idol.
history: Established in 1542, this is one of the most prestigious temples of the 'Gaudiya Vaishnava' tradition. It was founded by Gopal Bhatta Goswami. The temple is unique because the kitchen fire here has been burning continuously for over 450 years since the temple's inception; no matches are ever used. The deity of Radha Raman is self-manifested from a 'Shaligram Shila' (sacred stone) and is considered one of the most beautiful idols in Vrindavan.
mythology: Gopal Bhatta Goswami used to worship 12 Shaligram Shilas. He once expressed a desire to worship the Lord in a human-like form with beautiful features. On the full moon day of Vaishakha, one of the Shaligram Shilas, the 'Damodar Shila,' miraculously transformed into the deity of Radha Raman. Because the idol appeared on its own to fulfill the devotee's desire, it is considered a living manifestation of Krishna's love.

name: Govind Dev Ji Temple
description: Located in Jaipur City Palace complex, originally from Vrindavan.
history: The idol of Govind Dev Ji was originally installed in a magnificent seven-story red sandstone temple in Vrindavan, built by Raja Man Singh of Amer. However, during the reign of Aurangzeb, to protect the idol from destruction, it was moved to Jaipur. Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh II installed the deity in his palace gardens (Jai Niwas) in 1735. The temple is designed such that the Maharaja could see the deity directly from his palace, symbolizing that the Lord is the true King of Jaipur.
mythology: It is believed that the idol of Govind Dev Ji looks exactly like Lord Krishna during his earthly stay. According to legend, Krishna’s grandson, Vajranabha, asked his mother what the Lord looked like. Based on her description, he carved three idols. Govind Dev Ji is said to have the face that perfectly matches Krishna’s face. The idol is central to the life of Jaipur, and the city’s culture revolves around the daily 'sevas' of the Lord.

name: Udupi Sri Krishna Temple
description: Founded by philosopher Madhvacharya, idol worshipped through Navagraha Kindi window.
history: The Udupi Sri Krishna Matha was established by the great philosopher Saint Madhvacharya in the 13th century. The temple is unique because the deity is worshipped through a silver-plated window with nine holes, known as the 'Kanakana Kindi.' The administration of the temple rotates among eight monasteries (Ashta Mathas) every two years in a grand ceremony called 'Paryaya,' a tradition that has continued for over 700 years.
mythology: The idol is said to have been made by Vishwakarma and was worshipped by Rukmini in Dwarka. After Dwarka sank, the idol was covered in 'Gopichandan' clay and was being carried by a merchant in a ship. Saint Madhvacharya sensed the divine presence, saved the ship from a storm, and discovered the idol. Another famous legend is about Kanakadasa, a lower-caste devotee who was denied entry. He prayed so hard that the idol turned around and the temple wall cracked, creating a window so he could see his Lord.

name: Radha Rani Temple
description: Dedicated to Radha, birthplace of Radha, famous for Lathmar Holi festival.
history: The Radha Rani Temple, also known as Shriji Temple, is located on the Bhanugarh hill in Barsana. The current temple was built by King Veer Singh in the 17th century using red and white stones. The temple is reached by climbing over 200 steps. Barsana is the center of the famous 'Lathmar Holi' celebrations. Historically, the temple has been the focal point of the Braj region's culture, celebrating the feminine aspect of the divine with great pomp and ancient folk traditions.
mythology: Barsana is the birthplace of Shri Radha, the eternal consort of Krishna. Mythology says that the four hills of Barsana represent the four heads of Lord Brahma, who wished to take the form of mountains to witness the pastimes of Radha and Krishna. The temple is located where Radha’s father, Vrishabhanu, had his palace. Devotees believe that Radha is the soul of Krishna, and a pilgrimage to Braj is incomplete without seeking her blessings in Barsana.

name: Nand Bhavan Temple
description: Residence of Nanda Maharaj, foster father of Krishna, important stop in Braj pilgrimage.
history: Nand Bhavan is located on the Nandishwar hill in Nandgaon. The temple was built in the 19th century by Raja Roop Singh on the site where Nanda Maharaja, the foster father of Krishna, is believed to have lived. The temple architecture is simple but grand, reflecting a palace-like structure. It serves as the counterpart to Barsana, and every year during Holi, the men from Nandgaon go to Barsana to play Holi, maintaining a centuries-old cultural bond between the two villages.
mythology: After moving from Gokul to escape Kansa's demons, Nanda Maharaja and the residents of Vraja settled on this hill. It is here that Krishna spent his late childhood and adolescence. The hill is believed to be an incarnation of Lord Shiva, who wished to be under the feet of Krishna. The temple houses beautiful idols of Nanda, Yashoda, Krishna, and Balarama, and the atmosphere remains filled with the 'Vatsalya Bhava' (parental love for the divine).

name: Radha Damodar Temple
description: Houses sacred relics of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's followers, with Govardhan Hill footprint.
history: The Radha Damodar Temple was established in 1542 by Srila Jiva Goswami. During the 17th-century invasion by Aurangzeb, the original deity was moved to Jaipur for safety, where it remains today, while a 'Pratibhu-murti' (replica) is worshipped in Vrindavan. The temple complex is historically significant because it houses the 'Bhajan Kutir' and 'Samadhi' of many great saints, including Jiva Goswami and A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, who stayed here before traveling to the West to spread Krishna consciousness.
mythology: The temple's most sacred treasure is a 'Shaligram Shila' that was given to Srila Jiva Goswami by his guru, Rupa Goswami. Legend says that Lord Krishna himself appeared and gave this stone to Rupa Goswami, and it still bears the footprint of Krishna, a hoof-print of a cow, and a mark of a flute. It is believed that performing four parikramas (circumambulations) of this temple is equivalent to performing a parikrama of the entire Govardhan Hill.

name: Govardhan Nath Temple
description: Located near Govardhan Hill, pilgrims perform 21 km circumambulation (Parikrama).
history: The idol of Shri Nathji was originally worshipped at Govardhan Hill near Mathura. However, in 1672, to protect the deity from the iconoclastic campaigns of Aurangzeb, the idol was moved toward Rajasthan. Legend says that the wheels of the chariot carrying the Lord got stuck in the mud at a village called Sihar. Taking this as a divine sign, a grand temple was built there, which is now the famous town of Nathdwara. The temple is managed like a royal palace, and the deity is treated as the 'King' of the region.
mythology: Shri Nathji represents the 7-year-old form of Lord Krishna when he lifted the Govardhan Hill on his little finger to protect the people of Vraja from the wrath of Indra. The idol is carved from a single block of black marble and depicts Krishna with his left hand raised. Devotees believe that the Lord actually resides in the temple, which is why the 'Ashta-chhap' poets wrote thousands of songs to describe his daily activities, from waking up to going to sleep.

name: ISKCON Krishna Balaram Temple
description: Famous for daily kirtan and cultural programs centered on Krishna devotion.
history: The Krishna Balaram Mandir was inaugurated in 1975 by A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, the founder of ISKCON. Built with pure white marble, it was the first major international temple in Vrindavan. It was designed to fulfill Prabhupada's vision of a place where people from all over the world could come to learn about the teachings of the Bhagavad Gita. Today, it is one of the most popular temples in India, famous for its high standards of cleanliness, 24-hour kirtan, and its beautiful guesthouse and library.
mythology: The temple is built on the 'Ramana Reti' area, which mythology identifies as the spot where Krishna and Balarama used to play in the soft white sands with their cowherd friends. Unlike most Vrindavan temples that focus only on Radha-Krishna, the main altar here features Krishna and Balarama together, celebrating the eternal bond between the two brothers. The spiritual atmosphere is meant to transport the devotee back to the 'Dvapara Yuga,' where the Lord performed his childhood pastimes.

name: Radhavallabh Temple
description: Dedicated to Lord Krishna, famous for traditional rituals and daily worship.
history: The Radha Vallabh Temple was founded by Shri Hith Harivansh Mahaprabhu in the 16th century. The old temple building, made of red sandstone, is a protected historical monument and is considered a masterpiece of medieval temple architecture. The new temple houses the same ancient deity. This temple is the center of the 'Radha Vallabh Sampradaya,' which emphasizes 'Raganuga Bhakti' or spontaneous love. The temple is unique because there is no separate idol of Radha; instead, a crown is placed next to Krishna to signify her presence.
mythology: According to the sect's traditions, the deity of Radha Vallabh was given to Hith Harivansh by a Brahmin in an area called Atalla. The mythology emphasizes that Radha and Krishna are one soul in two bodies. In this temple, the primary focus is on the 'Nitya Vihara'—the eternal, blissful pastimes of the divine couple in the groves of Vrindavan. The Lord is worshipped through elaborate music, poetry, and 'Shringar' (decoration), reflecting the sweet and aesthetic nature of divine love.

name: Tirupati Balaji Temple
description: One of the richest and most visited Vishnu temples in the world.
history: The Venkateswara Temple on Tirumala hills is the world's richest temple. It has been patronized by dynasties like the Pallavas, Cholas, and Vijayanagara Empire. Maharaja Krishnadevaraya was a great devotee and donated massive amounts of gold for the temple's Vimana. The temple has a meticulously documented history of grants and rituals inscribed on its walls, and today it manages one of the most complex and large-scale pilgrim management systems in the world.
mythology: Lord Vishnu appeared as Venkateswara to stay on earth until the end of Kali Yuga. To pay for his grand wedding with Princess Padmavati, he took a massive loan from Kubera. It is believed that devotees donate money and hair to the temple to help the Lord repay this debt. Mythology says that the Lord’s eyes are covered because his gaze is too powerful for humans to bear directly. The idol is self-manifested and is considered a 'living' form of the Lord.

name: Padmanabhaswamy Temple
description: Known for enormous treasure discovered in underground vaults.
history: The Sree Padmanabhaswamy Temple is known as the wealthiest religious institution in the world. While the foundations are ancient, the current massive structure and its 100-foot tall 'Gopuram' were built in the 16th century by the kings of the Travancore Royal Family. The temple is famous for its strict dress code and rituals. In 2011, it gained global attention when secret underground vaults (Vault B and others) were opened, revealing a vast treasure of gold, diamonds, and precious stones worth billions of dollars, preserved for centuries as the property of the deity.
mythology: The temple is dedicated to Lord Vishnu in the 'Anantha Shayanam' posture, reclining on the infinite serpent Anantha. According to legend, a great sage named Vilvamangalam Swamiyar prayed for a vision of the Lord. Vishnu appeared as a small boy and later led the sage to a forest where he transformed into a massive form. At the sage's request, the Lord shrunk his size to three times the length of the sage's staff, which is the exact size of the idol seen today. The city of Thiruvananthapuram literally means 'The City of Lord Anantha'.

name: Konark Sun Temple
description: Designed as a massive stone chariot with wheels and horses pulling the Sun God.
history: Built in the 13th century by King Narasimhadeva I of the Eastern Ganga Dynasty, the Konark Sun Temple is a UNESCO World Heritage site. It was designed as a colossal chariot for the Sun God, Surya, with 24 beautifully carved stone wheels and 7 galloping horses. Historically known as the 'Black Pagoda' by European sailors, the temple was once much larger, but its main tower collapsed over time. The stones are held together by iron beams, and it remains one of the greatest examples of Kalinga architecture and ancient Indian engineering.
mythology: According to the Puranas, Samba, the son of Lord Krishna, was cursed with leprosy. He performed severe penance for twelve years at the mouth of the Chandrabhaga River to please the Sun God, Surya, who was known as the healer of all diseases. Surya was pleased and cured him. In gratitude, Samba built this temple to honor the Sun God. The 24 wheels of the temple are said to represent the 24 hours of the day, and the intricate carvings act as a sundial, telling the exact time by the position of the shadows.

name: Martand Sun Temple
description: Built by the Karkota dynasty and now an important archaeological site.
history: The Martand Sun Temple was built in the 8th century CE by King Lalitaditya Muktapida of the Karkota Dynasty. It was once a magnificent structure that blended Kashmiri architectural styles with Greek and Roman influences. It sits on a plateau overlooking the Kashmir Valley. Unfortunately, the temple was destroyed in the 15th century by Sultan Sikandar Shah Miri. Today, its grand ruins are protected by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and continue to attract tourists and historians as a symbol of the region's glorious Hindu past.
mythology: The temple is dedicated to 'Martand,' which is another name for the Sun God (Surya) in his Vedic form. The word 'Martand' means 'Dead Egg,' referring to the legend of the birth of the Sun God as the eighth son of Aditi. It is believed that the site was chosen because of its spiritual energy and its height, which allowed the first rays of the sun to hit the temple. Local legends say that the Pandavas also performed rituals here during their exile, making the site sacred for thousands of years.

name: Hemkund Sahib
description: A Sikh-Hindu shared sacred site high in the Garhwal Himalayas. Pilgrims trek through scenic mountains to reach the Gurudwara.
history: Hemkund Sahib is a prominent Sikh pilgrimage site located at an altitude of 15,000 feet in the Himalayas. For centuries, the site remained hidden due to its difficult terrain. It was rediscovered in the 20th century by Sohan Singh and Modan Singh based on the descriptions in the 'Bachitar Natak,' the autobiography of Guru Gobind Singh. The current star-shaped Gurudwara was built in the 1960s. It is only accessible for five months a year (May to October) because it remains completely covered in snow during the winter.
mythology: According to the 'Bachitar Natak,' Guru Gobind Singh, the tenth Sikh Guru, mediated in his previous life on the shores of a lake surrounded by seven snow-capped peaks (Sapt Shring). He performed intense penance here to merge with the Divine. The site is also linked to Hindu mythology; it is believed that Lakshmana, the brother of Lord Rama, performed penance here to regain his strength after being wounded in the war against Ravana. The nearby 'Lokpal' temple is dedicated to him, showing the site's importance to both Sikhs and Hindus.
